title: Isaiah 63
subtitle: Who Is This Coming from Edom?

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “I Have Trodden the Winepress Alone”

heb.paragraph: "Who is this coming from Edom, from Bozrah, with his garments stained crimson? I have trodden the winepress (gat) alone; from the nations no one was with me" (vv.1, 3). The divine warrior returns from battle, clothes soaked in the blood of nations. "I trampled them in my anger and trod them down in my wrath; their blood spattered my garments" (v.3). Revelation 19:13–15 develops this directly: Christ returns with a robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress of God’s fury. "In my wrath I trampled them and their blood ran in the ground" (v.6). The ferocity is deliberate: the God who comforts (ch.40) also crushes (ch.63). Both are the same love.

notes.note: "Who is this from Edom, garments stained crimson?" The question-and-answer format: who? The warrior answers: I, who speak in righteousness, mighty to save. The blood is not his own but the nations.
"I have trodden the winepress alone; from the nations no one was with me." Alone: no human ally, no angelic army. The solitary warrior. Rev 19:13–15 applies this to Christ’s return.
"The day of vengeance was in my heart, and the year for my redemption had come." The vengeance that Jesus omitted at Nazareth (61:2b) now arrives. The comma is closed.

refs.note: The rider on the white horse with robe dipped in blood treading the winepress directly draws on Isaiah's Edom oracle.
The great winepress of God's wrath producing blood reaching horse bridles develops Isaiah's imagery.
Joel's command to tread the winepress because the vat overflows parallels Isaiah's solo judgment.

hist.context: The warrior from Edom with garments stained like a grape-treader represents divine judgment on Israel's ancient enemy. Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall (Ps 137:7; Obadiah) made it symbolic of all hostile nations. The winepress of wrath became standard apocalyptic imagery, with Isaiah's lone warrior anticipating later christological appropriation.

heb.paragraph: "You, LORD, are our Father (ʾābînū). Our Redeemer from of old is your name" (v.16). The tone shifts entirely: from divine warrior to communal lament. "I will tell of the kindnesses of the LORD, the deeds for which he is to be praised" (v.7). God became their Saviour; in all their distress he too was distressed (vv.8–9). But they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit (v.10) — Paul echoes this in Ephesians 4:30: "Do not grieve the Holy Spirit." Where is your zeal and your might? Your tenderness and compassion are withheld from us (v.15). "Though Abraham does not know us and Israel does not acknowledge us, you, LORD, are our Father" (v.16). The appeal bypasses human ancestry to claim divine parentage directly.

notes.note: "In all their distress he too was distressed, and the angel of his presence saved them." God’s empathy: when they hurt, he hurts. Not distant sovereignty but shared suffering.
"They rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit." Eph 4:30: "Do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God." The Spirit is personal enough to grieve. Isaiah names the Spirit as person, not force.
"Though Abraham does not know us, you, LORD, are our Father." The radical move: bypassing the patriarchs to address God directly as Father. Human lineage fails; divine parentage endures.

refs.note: Paul's Abba Father language recalls Isaiah's repeated appeal to God as father.
The Spirit crying 'Abba Father' develops the intimate divine paternity Isaiah invokes.
The Lord's Prayer opening 'Our Father' stands in Isaiah's tradition of addressing God as father.

hist.context: The shift from judgment (vv.1-6) to lament (vv.7-19) is jarring but theologically coherent: the same God who tramples enemies is invoked as Father and Redeemer. The appeal to Abraham and Israel not recognizing their descendants reflects post-exilic alienation. The cry 'Why do you harden our hearts?' anticipates later debates about divine sovereignty and human responsibility.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "Though Abraham does not know us, you, LORD, are our Father…

ppl.name: nations
ppl.text: The blood is not his own but the nations…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI will tell of the kindnesses of the Lord, the deeds for which he is to be praised, according to all the Lord has done for us— yes, the many good things he has done for Israel, according to his compassion and many kindnesses.ESVI will recount the steadfast love of the LORD, the praises of the LORD, according to all that the LORD has granted us, and the great goodness to the house of Israel that he has granted them according to his compassion, according to the abundance of his steadfast love.NIVI trampled the nations in my anger; in my wrath I made them drunk and poured their blood on the ground.”ESVI trampled down the peoples in my anger; I made them drunk in my wrath, and I poured out their lifeblood on the earth.\u201d

rec.text: ""Who is this from Edom, garments stained crimson?" The question-and-answer format: who? The warrior answers: I, who speak in righteousness, mighty to save. The blood is not his own but the nations."

rows.label: Our Father — 63:7–19
rows.text: "You, LORD, are our FATHER." God distressed in their distress. "They GRIEVED his Holy Spirit" (Eph 4:30).

hebtext.note: "Who is this coming from Edom, from Bozrah, with his garments stained crimson? I have trodden the winepress (gat) alone; from the nations no one was with me" (vv.1, 3). The divine warrior returns from battle, clothes soaked in the blood of nations. "I trampled them in my anger and trod them down in my wrath; their blood spattered my garments" (v.3). Revelation 19:13–15 develops this directly: Christ returns with a robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress of God’s fury. "In my wrath I trampled them and their blood ran in the ground" (v.6). The ferocity is deliberate: the God who comforts (ch.40) also crushes (ch.63). Both are the same love.

"You, LORD, are our Father (ʾābînū). Our Redeemer from of old is your name" (v.16). The tone shifts entirely: from divine warrior to communal lament. "I will tell of the kindnesses of the LORD, the deeds for which he is to be praised" (v.7). God became their Saviour; in all their distress he too was distressed (vv.8–9). But they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit (v.10) — Paul echoes this in Ephesians 4:30: "Do not grieve the Holy Spirit." Where is your zeal and your might? Your tenderness and compassion are withheld from us (v.15). "Though Abraham does not know us and Israel does not acknowledge us, you, LORD, are our Father" (v.16). The appeal bypasses human ancestry to claim divine parentage directly.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Prophecy peak at 9: the winepress warrior (Rev 19:13), the Holy Spirit grieved (Eph 4:30), God as Father. Judgment at 8: the vengeance Jesus omitted now arrives.

coaching.tip: 'I have trodden the winepress alone; from the nations no one was with me' (v. 3). The warrior-God returns from battle, garments stained with the blood of enemies. Revelation 19:13–15 applies this image to Christ at his return. The winepress of divine judgment — solitary, decisive, total — connects Isaiah's warrior to John's rider on the white horse. The image is violent because the evil it addresses is real.

'You, LORD, are our Father' (v. 16). This is one of the rare Old Testament texts that addresses God as Father — alongside Deuteronomy 32:6, Psalm 68:5, and Malachi 2:10. Isaiah grounds the appeal in divine fatherhood when human ancestry fails: 'Though Abraham does not know us, you, LORD, are our Father.' When every other identity marker is stripped away, one remains: God's parentage.
